html:
Repairing Hail and Wind Damage on a Springfield Commercial Roof

Southwest Missouri sits in a stretch of the country that gets more hail activity than most people realize until it hits their own roof. Spring and early summer storm cells moving through the Ozarks can drop hail large enough to bruise a commercial membrane, crack pipe boots, and dent rooftop equipment, sometimes without an owner noticing until months later when a slow leak finally shows up inside the building.

What Hail Actually Does to a Commercial Membrane

Hail impact on a single-ply membrane doesn't always punch straight through. More often it bruises the membrane — compressing the material enough to weaken it at the impact point without an immediate hole. That bruise becomes a weak spot that fails months or years later under normal UV exposure and thermal cycling, which is why a roof can look fine right after a storm and still be carrying damage that shortens its service life. On gravel-surfaced built-up roofs, hail can displace the ballast entirely and expose the membrane below to direct impact it wasn't designed to take.

We check soft metal on the roof — pipe boot collars, HVAC housing, gutter and downspout material — for hail dents as a proxy for impact size across the field, since that's often easier to measure than bruising on the membrane itself.

Consistent dent size and spacing across the soft metal tells us the storm hit the whole roof evenly. Dents concentrated on one side of the building point to a storm that moved through at an angle, which usually means membrane damage is concentrated there too rather than spread evenly across the field.

Wind Damage: Uplift Stress Beyond the Missing Material

Missing membrane or a torn section is the obvious wind damage. The less obvious kind is uplift damage where wind has gotten under a seam or fastening point and stressed it without fully tearing it loose. That kind of damage can look fine from a walk-through and still fail the next time wind hits from a similar direction. We check field seams and perimeter fastening specifically after any wind event strong enough to have caused uplift, beyond just the areas with visible tears.

Why Storm Repair Timing Matters

Repairing storm damage promptly limits how much water gets into insulation and deck before the fix happens, and it keeps the documentation timeline clean if a claim is involved — damage assessed and repaired close to the event date is easier to tie back to that specific storm than damage addressed months later after additional weather has had a chance to compound it.

Waiting also stacks risk. A roof carrying hail bruising or a wind-stressed seam from one storm is more vulnerable in the next one, and this region rarely gets just a single severe weather event in a season. Repair timing matters as much for what's coming next as for what already happened.

  • Full field inspection for membrane bruising, not only the area with an active leak
  • Soft metal check for hail dents to gauge impact size across the roof
  • Seam and perimeter fastening check after wind events
  • Photo documentation dated close to the storm event
  • Written repair scope separating storm-caused damage from pre-existing wear

Storm-Chaser Crews and What to Watch For

After a significant hailstorm, this region sees crews come through from out of state going door to door with roof damage claims and pressure to sign a contract on the spot, sometimes before an owner has even confirmed there's actual damage. Some of that work is legitimate. A lot of it is priced to move fast on volume rather than to do a careful repair, and crews that aren't local often aren't around a year later if the repair doesn't hold. We'd rather an owner get a second opinion before signing anything with a crew they can't verify has a track record in this market.

Repair vs. Replacement After a Significant Storm

Not every hail event calls for full replacement, and we don't default to recommending one. A roof with isolated damage on an otherwise sound, newer membrane is usually a repair candidate. A roof that was already near the end of its service life before the storm, or one where hail damage is widespread across the field, is a different conversation — one where repair might cost nearly as much as replacement without extending the roof's life by much.

We put both numbers on paper when the call is close — repair cost against remaining life on one side, replacement cost against a full new warranty term on the other — so the owner is deciding with real figures instead of a single recommendation with no comparison behind it.

Storm Damage Repair Questions

How do I know if my roof has hail damage if there's no active leak?

Bruising and soft-metal dents aren't always visible from the ground. An on-roof inspection after a known storm event is the only reliable way to check.

Should I call my insurance company before or after an inspection?

Either order can work, but having a documented, dated inspection strengthens whatever claim conversation follows.

Can you tell how old hail damage is?

We can often distinguish recent impact marks from older, weathered damage, though exact dating isn't always possible from the roof alone.

Is all hail damage covered by insurance?

That depends on the policy and the adjuster's assessment — we document conditions but don't make coverage determinations ourselves.

How do I know if a storm-repair crew is legitimate?

Ask for local references, a physical business address, and a written scope before signing anything, especially with crews that appeared right after a storm.
